metadata.summary: A 9-phase structured process for writing spec.md with SMART functional requirements, user stories, acceptance criteria, and explicit scope boundaries to prevent scope creep and establish a single written source of truth before design begins.

content.title: Writing Specifications
content.summary: A structured 9-phase process for writing spec.md — the document that answers "WHAT are we building and WHY?" before design or implementation begins. Covers problem analysis, user personas, user story mapping, SMART functional and non-functional requirements, Given-When-Then acceptance criteria, and explicit scope boundaries.

why.p: Projects fail when teams build the wrong thing because requirements are vague, stakeholders have different mental models, or there is no single written source of truth. A spec written with SMART criteria, FR traceability, and testable ACs reduces this risk: every FR has an ID, every AC has a pass/fail test, and out-of-scope items are explicitly named — preventing scope creep during design.

list.item: Feature is new and requirements have not been written down anywhere.
Existing feature needs scope expansion and the current spec is absent or too vague to drive design.
Stakeholder and developer have different mental models of what the feature does.
Requirements exist informally (Slack messages, verbal agreements) and need to be formalized.
Constitution is new and needs to capture tech decisions before development begins.
Bug report with a clear reproduction path — write a task directly, not a spec.
Infrastructure change (server config, deployment pipeline) with no user-visible behavior.
Feature already has an approved spec — open and amend it rather than rewriting from scratch.
Experiment/spike where the output will determine whether to proceed at all.

section.name: document-hierarchy
section.p: A specification answers WHAT and WHY. A design answers HOW. An implementation plan answers IN WHAT ORDER. Never mix concerns across documents.

rule.statement: A specification answers WHAT and WHY. A design answers HOW. An implementation plan answers IN WHAT ORDER. Never mix concerns across documents.
rule.rationale: Mixing implementation details into spec.md causes the spec to become stale when the design changes, and breaks the clean hand-off between phases.

example.title: Document responsibility table
example.code: Specification → WHAT and WHY? → Requirements, user stories, acceptance criteria Design → HOW? → Architecture, file structure, API contracts Impl Plan → IN WHAT ORDER? → Tasks, dependencies, token estimates

rule.statement: Load constitution.md and completed feature specs BEFORE writing any requirements.
rule.rationale: Requirements written without constitution context violate tech stack constraints. Reviewing done/ features surfaces patterns and prevents re-inventing resolved problems.

list.item: Phase 1: Load context — read constitution.md, features/done/, related specs in todo/in-progress/
Phase 2: Problem analysis — define WHO, WHAT, WHY, IMPACT using SMART criteria
Phase 3: User research — define minimum 2 personas with role, goal, pain points, context
Phase 4: User story mapping — backbone, walking skeleton (MVP), phase 2 stories
Phase 5: Use cases — for multi-step workflows, multiple actors, or complex validations
Phase 6: Functional requirements — SMART requirements with MoSCoW, FR-X IDs, US traceability
Phase 7: Non-functional requirements — measurable targets (no vague words)
Phase 8: Acceptance criteria — Given-When-Then covering all scenarios with specific values
Phase 9: Scope definition — explicitly list what IS and IS NOT in scope

rule.statement: Every functional requirement must satisfy SMART: Specific (one interpretation), Measurable (verifiable), Achievable (technically feasible), Relevant (traces to user need), and have a testable pass/fail criterion.
rule.rationale: Vague requirements cannot be tested. "System should be fast" is untestable; "response time < 500ms p95" is not.

example.title: Vague requirement — fails SMART
example.code: FR-001: System should be fast and handle user requests efficiently.

example.title: SMART requirement
example.code: FR-001: System SHALL return API responses within 500ms p95 under 100 concurrent users. [Must]

rule.statement: Every user story must include the "so that [benefit]" clause. A story without a benefit clause hides the value and cannot be prioritized.
rule.rationale: The benefit clause is where business value lives. Stories without it get implemented based on assumed value, which is frequently wrong.

example.title: Missing benefit clause
example.code: US-001: As a user, I want to log in with my email.

example.title: Complete user story
example.code: US-001: As a registered user, I want to log in with my email and password, so that I can access my personal account and saved data.

antipattern.description: "System should be fast" as a non-functional requirement.
antipattern.reason: Not measurable. Rewrite as: "Response time < 500ms p95".
Requirements written without personas are not grounded in user context and tend to describe system behavior rather than user needs.
Unidentified requirements cannot be traced to design decisions or acceptance criteria.

antipattern.description: Out of Scope section is empty or lists only obvious exclusions.
antipattern.reason: Out of Scope must name specific features that were discussed and explicitly rejected; generic exclusions do not prevent scope creep.

antipattern.description: AC items are not testable: "system should handle errors gracefully".
antipattern.reason: Acceptance criteria without Given-When-Then and specific values cannot be verified by a test.

list.item: Read .aidocs/constitution.md — project principles and tech stack
Review features/done/ for completed feature patterns
Identify related specs in features/todo/ or features/in-progress/
Extract tech stack constraints and code standards
Note existing patterns and architecture decisions
Define problem with WHO/WHAT/WHY/IMPACT clearly
Apply SMART criteria: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Create business value statement linking user need to impact
Define success metric that measures outcome
Define minimum 2 user personas with role, goal, pain points, context
Map user activities and create story mapping backbone
Identify walking skeleton (MVP) vs phase 2 stories
Validate personas with stakeholders
Write user stories in "As a...I want...so that" format
Ensure each story follows INVEST principle
Create functional requirements (FR-X) with MoSCoW prioritization
Define non-functional requirements (NFR-X) with specific numeric targets
Ensure all requirements are SMART and testable
Write Given-When-Then scenarios covering happy path
Write error handling scenarios
Write boundary condition scenarios
Use specific values — not "valid" or "fast"
Ensure criteria are testable with pass/fail verification
Explicitly list what IS in scope
Explicitly list what is NOT in scope with reason and timeline
Define assumptions about user behavior and system constraints
Identify dependencies on other features or systems
Verify all sections are complete (problem, personas, stories, FR, NFR, AC, scope)
Check for ambiguous language (good, fast, easy, etc.)
Validate traceability: each FR traces to a user story
Confirm requirements are SMART and not solution-focused
Ensure out-of-scope section names specific rejected features

rule.statement: Spec must reach Approved status before design begins. Never hand a Draft spec to an executor agent.
rule.rationale: Design decisions made against unapproved requirements cause rework when requirements change during review.

list.item: Present specification to stakeholders for feedback
Resolve open questions and gaps
Update spec based on feedback
Obtain formal approval before moving to design phase

template.purpose: Shell script to create the .aidocs/features/backlog/NN-slug/ directory and seed a blank spec.md.
